title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202601019H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-03412

CPA HEARING NO. 120,183

 


RE:      COMPANY A


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:  January 1, 2019 THROUGH October 31, 2021


Sales And Use Tax/RFD


 

 


SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-03413

CPA HEARING NO. 120,184

 


RE:      COMPANY B


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:  January 1, 2019 THROUGH October 31, 2021


Sales And Use Tax/RFD

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


ALBERTO CRUZ

Representing Respondent


INDIVIDUAL A

Representing Claimant


 

 
COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on February 17, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as changed.[2] 


The results from this Decision are Attachments A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as changed is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachments A reflect credits owed to Claimants.


The credit will be processed after the date the comptroller’s decision is final. The parties may waive the right to file a motion for rehearing to expedite the processing of the credit. A waiver of rehearing or motion for rehearing may be filed as described above.


SIGNED on this 22nd day of January 2026

 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachments A, Texas Notifications of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as changed

 

 

 



ATTACHMENT B

 

 


State Office of Administrative Hearings

Kristofer S. Monson 

Chief Administrative Law Judge


 

August 26, 2025


 


Alberto Cruz                                                             

Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

INDIVIDUAL A                                                           

For Claimants

 

RE: SOAH Docket Nos. 304-25-03412.26 and 304-25-03413.26; 

Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts Nos. 120,183 and 120,184

 

 

Dear Parties:

 

The undersigned Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) regarding the above referenced matters on July 18, 2025. The ALJ granted Claimants’ request to extend the exceptions period. Claimants timely filed exceptions to the PFD on August 8, 2025, and Staff timely filed a response to Claimants’ exceptions on August 25, 2025. The ALJ reviewed the exceptions and the response and recommends that the exceptions be rejected. Claimants contend in their exceptions that the COMPANY C system is eligible for the manufacturing exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2),(a)(4). Though Claimants cite to additional legal authorities to support their arguments, Claimants’ exceptions reassert arguments made in the hearing. Having reviewed the filings of the parties, and finding no errors in the findings of fact or conclusions of law, I recommend that the PFD be adopted as written.

 

Signed August 26, 2025

 


Keneshia Washington,

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket Nos. 304-25-03412; 304-25-03413                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing Nos. 120,183 and 120,184

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

COMPANY A (Claimant 1) and COMPANY B (Claimant 2) (collectively “Claimants”) contest the denial of their refund requests. The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) denied the refund claims on the grounds that the documentation provided did not demonstrate that tax was paid in error. Claimants requested a refund hearing, contending they erroneously remitted tax to their vendors for components of a utility-scale photovoltaic solar power station. At the hearing Claimants withdrew their contention regarding the exemption for temporary storage in Texas. Staff agreed to a partial refund; however, disputes remain. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) finds that the claim for the remaining refund amounts must be denied because record evidence does not establish that the remaining contested items are eligible for the manufacturing exemption or are otherwise tax exempt. Therefore, the ALJ recommends that the partial denial of the refund claim be affirmed, except as agreed by Staff.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on October 22, 2024, ALJ Keneshia Washington issued an Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits, setting out the pre-hearing requirements. On October 30, 2024, Staff issued Claimants a Notice of Hearing. After continuances, the hearing convened in person at the SOAH offices in Austin, Texas on April 29, 2025. Alberto Cruz represented Staff, and INDIVIDUAL B and INDIVIDUAL A of COMPANY D represented Claimants. The record closed on May 20, 2025, after Staff and Claimants filed written closing arguments, a refund schedule indicating agreed amounts and remaining amounts in contention for each hearing, and their exhibits admitted to the record during the hearing via Texas E-File.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion here.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence Presented

 

Staff provided the pleadings the parties exchanged prior to referring the matter to SOAH. Staff offered the testimony of auditor Liliana Huerta and the following exhibits:


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Refund Denial;
	
	
	Audit Plan; [ENDNOTE 3]
	
	
	Claimant 1 Refund Schedule; and
	
	
	Claimant 2 Refund Schedule.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits 1 – 3 were admitted to the record over Claimants’ hearsay objection. The ALJ considered the objection when determining how much weight to give the testimony and exhibits admitted. Exhibits 4 and 5 were admitted without objection.

 

Claimants offered the testimony of Claimant 2’s Director of Operations and Maintenance, INDIVIDUAL C, and CHAIR of the CHEMICAL ENGINNEERING DEPARTMENT at UNIVERSITY Professor INDIVIDUAL D as well as the following exhibits:


	
	Ground Level Still Photos of COMPANY A Power Project;
	
	
	Aerial Still Photos of COMPANY A Power Project;
	
	
	Time Lapse Video of COMPANY A Panels being Rotated by Tracker System;
	
	
	Aerial Video of COMPANY A Panels and Tracker System;
	
	
	Declaration of INDIVIDUAL E Authenticating Exhibits 1 through 4, above;
	
	
	Graph of April 8, 2025, Readings from COMPANY A Power Plant Showing Light Absorption from Fixed Angles Versus Tracker Panels;
	
	
	April 8, 2025, Readings from COMPANY A Power Plant Showing Light Absorption from Fixed Angles Versus Tracker Panels (Spreadsheet);
	
	
	COMPANY C “************** Whitepaper;”
	
	
	COMPANY C “************** Features” Sheet;
	
	
	COMPANY C “Solar Trackers: Active Energy Generating Systems on Power Plants” Presentation;
	
	
	2017 National Electric Code Excerpt;
	
	
	COMPANY C Invoices for Materials Purchased;
	
	
	COMPANY C Invoices for Sales and Use Tax Charges;
	
	
	Spreadsheet of Equipment Totals Taken from Petitioners’ Accounting Records and COMPANY C Invoices;
	
	
	Spreadsheet re COMPANY A COMPANY C Bill of Materials List;
	
	
	Spreadsheet re: TX Refund Request Reconciliation Taken from Petitioners’ Accounting and COMPANY C;
	
	
	Assignment of Right to Refund from COMPANY C, to COMPANY E;
	
	
	Engineering, Procurement, and Construction Agreement;
	
	
	COMPANY A Texas Claim for Refund;
	
	
	COMPANY E Texas Claim for Refund;
	
	
	Statement Regarding Entitlement to Sales Tax Refund;
	
	
	COMPANY A Claim Denial RE: Limited Sales, Excise, and Use Tax Refund Request;
	
	
	COMPANY E Claim Denial RE: Limited Sales, Excise and Use Tax Refund Request;
	
	
	Safe Harbor Product Supply Agreement #2 by and Between COMPANY B d/b/a COMPANY E and COMPANY C, Dated as of September 6, 2019;
	
	
	Letter from INDIVIDUAL G to COMPANY C re Buyer Taxes Dated April 27, 2020;
	
	
	Letter Disputing Invoices from INDIVIDUAL G to COMPANY C Dated May 5, 2020;
	
	
	Expert Report of Dr. INDIVIDUAL D;
	
	
	The Administrative Hearing Section’s Response to Claimant’s Motion for Partial Summary Disposition;
	
	
	The Administrative Hearing Section’s Reply to Petitioners’ Discovery Requests;
	
	
	COMPANY A Sales and Use Tax Summary Table; and
	
	
	COMPANY E Sales and Use Tax Summary Table.
	


 

Claimants’ exhibits were admitted to the record without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff agreed to refund amounts reflected in Exhibits 4 and 5.

 

C. Facts Established and Issued Presented

 

Claimant 1 is a is a utility-scale solar power station in COUNTY, Texas, and Claimant 2 is its corporate affiliate. Claimants operate a commercial solar farm. Modules and semiconductors in the solar panels absorb sunlight and convert sunlight into electricity. The solar panels are assembled into an array with a tracker system and other equipment. Claimants’ solar farm contains multiple arrays. Claimants purchase components of the tracker system from its vendor, COMPANY C. The array is custom designed and assembled on site.

 

A tracker system is the grouping of components integrated into a solar array that allows the solar panels to move. The primary purpose of the movement is to maximize the exposure of the solar panels to sunlight, increasing the amount of sunlight converted into electricity by tracking the sun in the sky and adjusting the position accordingly. A secondary purpose is safety in that moving the solar panels enables protection during severe weather conditions, including hail, high winds, and flooding. COMPANY C uses latitude, longitude, and date to determine when the sun will rise and fall and what position would be optimal to increase irradiance.

 

Claimants requested a refund of sales and use tax erroneously paid on purchases to vendors, namely COMPANY C, for the period of January 1, 2019, through October 31, 2021. Staff conducted a verification of Claimants’ refund claim. The auditor reviewed Claimants’ purchase invoices, general ledger accounts, COMPANY C’s sales and use tax returns filed with the Comptroller. The auditor also reached out to COMPANY C for information regarding its sales to Claimants. In February 2023, Staff denied the refund claims in full. Claimants timely filed a request for a hearing, contending the items denied were eligible for the manufacturing exemption. Claimants also argue that they are due refund amounts for overpaid tax. Staff disagreed and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

A hearing on the merits convened, and Claimants presented the testimony of INDIVIDUAL C and Prof. INDIVIDUAL D. Mr. INDIVIDUAL C works as Claimant 2’s Director of Operations and Maintenance. Claimant 2 owns and operates several solar farms and Mr. INDIVIDUAL C manages third-party operators, on-site personnel, and work performed at the site. Mr. INDIVIDUAL C has worked in the solar farm industry for almost a decade and he developed solar farms from the early stages through construction. He has also owned and operated several of them. Mr. INDIVIDUAL C manages the COMPANY A project and checks production on it every morning. Professor INDIVIDUAL D is the CHAIR of the CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT at UNIVERSITY. He is the Director of the ENERGY INSTUTE.

 

Mr. INDIVIDUAL C and Prof. INDIVIDUAL D explained that the commercial solar farm is comprised of an assembly of rows of arrays, including solar panels, that would move in unison. Each row has its own controller and receives information from a COMPANY C control unit. They also explained that the position of the module in the solar panel is crucial to maximize output, or irradiance. A module pointing directly at the sun increases the footprint of irradiance. They further testified that the goal of the COMPANY C system is to make more electricity and that without a tracker system, the commercial solar farm would not be financially viable, and it would endure a higher cost to produce electricity on a per watt level.

 

Mr. INDIVIDUAL C, Prof. INDIVIDUAL D, and Staff auditor Liliana Huerta testified that the COMPANY C system facilitates movement of the solar panels and is not needed for the solar panels to function.

 

D. ALJ’s Analysis and Recommendation

 

If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a). In a contested case hearing, a Claimant must produce contemporaneous records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a); see also Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041. A taxpayer has the burden to prove error in an assessment or refund denial by a preponderance of the evidence. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).

 

A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions by source records, and factual assertions in pleadings are not evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,587 (2015) and 112,607 (2019); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 115,999 (2020), citing to Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.— Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,916 (2013).

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. When tax is imposed on tangible personal property the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,506 (2020).

 

A taxpayer has the burden to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the taxpayer or a transaction qualifies for an exemption. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c). Clear and convincing evidence demonstrates the thing to be proved is highly probable. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,773 (2011). Statutory exemptions are strictly construed and all doubts concerning exemption claims are resolved in favor of the taxing authority. See North Alamo Water Supply Corp. v. Willacy County Appraisal Dist., 804 S.W.2d 894, 899 (Tex. 1991); Sharp v. Tyler Pipe Indus. Inc., 919 S.W.2d 157, 161 (Tex. App.—Austin 1996, writ denied); Sharp v. Chevron Chem. Co., 924 S.W.2d 429, 432 (Tex. App.—Austin 1996, writ denied).

 

1. Texas Tax Code § 151.318(aA)(2)(A)

 

The statute provides an exemption for tangible personal property directly used or consumed in or during the actual manufacturing, processing, or fabrication of tangible personal property for ultimate sale if the use or consumption of the property is necessary or essential to the manufacturing, processing, or fabrication operation and directly makes or causes a chemical or physical change to the product being manufactured, processed, or fabricated for ultimate sale. Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2)(A).

 

Manufacturing includes each operation beginning with the first stage in the production of tangible personal property and ending with the completion of tangible personal property. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(9). Completion of production means the tangible personal property has all the physical properties, including packaging, if any, that it has when transferred by the manufacturer to another. Id. § 3.300(a)(9)(A). Processing and fabrication are two activities that are performed during manufacturing. Id. § 3.300(a)(9)(B). Processing is defined as “the physical application of the materials and labor necessary to modify or to change the characteristics of tangible personal property.” Id. § 3.300(a)(10). Fabrication is defined as “to make, build, create, produce, or assemble components of tangible personal property, or to make tangible personal property work in a new or different manner.” Id. § 3.300(a)(5).

 

The tangible personal property at issue must directly make or cause a chemical or physical change to the product being manufactured, processed, or fabricated for ultimate sale in order to be eligible for the manufacturing exemption under that section. Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2)(A). Based on record evidence, including the sworn testimony of the auditor, Mr. INDIVIDUAL C, and Prof. INDIVIDUAL D, the COMPANY C system is not involved in the process of converting sunlight into electric current. Though the tracker system maximizes the opportunity for the process to occur, it does not cause the process to occur. Because the COMPANY C System does not cause the conversion of sunlight into electricity, the COMPANY C System is not eligible for the manufacturing exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2)(A).

 

2. Solar Panel vs. Solar Array

 

The purchases at issue are part of the COMPANY C tracking system. None of the purchases are for a solar panel. The parties agree that the solar panel is a piece of manufacturing equipment used to convert sunlight into electricity. See State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document No. 201907005L (July 8, 2019) (explaining, “Solar modules/panels used to convert sunlight into electricity for ultimate sale qualify for exemption under Section 151.318(a)(2). They are single items of manufacturing equipment. Components of such modules/panels also qualify for exemption.”).

 

Based on record evidence, including expert and professional testimony at the hearing, the solar panel and the tracking system that positions each solar panel is referred to collectively as a solar array in the industry. Claimants contend the Comptroller should determine the eligibility of its COMPANY C purchases by applying Tax Code 151.318 and the corresponding Rules, Comptroller jurisprudence, and taxability guidance to the solar array as a single unit. To support its contention, Claimants reference the Texas Occupations code which states, “the executive director or commission, as appropriate, shall after publication of the National Electrical Code by the National Fire Protection Association every three years, adopt the revised National Electrical Code as the electrical code for the state.” Tex. Occupations Code § 1305.101(a)(2). Claimants cite to the National Electrical Code 2017 edition for the definition of an array as “[a] mechanically integrated assembly of module(s) or panel(s) with a support structure and foundation, tracker, and other components, as required, to form a dc or ac power- producing unit.” [ENDNOTE 4]

 

The Texas Tax Code does not require the Comptroller to adopt the definition of a solar array published in the National Electrical Code. The determination of whether the contested purchases of the COMPANY C system constitute a component part of manufacturing equipment is a fact-driven question. See, e.g. Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 111,253 (2017), 105,258 (2011), 44,820 (2005), and 44,299 (2006). Furthermore, as described in the next section below, applying manufacturing exemption analysis based on a collection of pieces of equipment is contrary to the Texas Tax Code, Rules, Comptroller jurisprudence, and guidance.

 

3. Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2) – Component Part

 

Although the statute does not contain the phrase “component part of the manufacturing equipment,” the agency construes Section 151.318(a)(2) as including the language. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,253 (2017); 44,820 (2005). In order for an item to qualify as a component part of manufacturing equipment, it must be reasonably essential to the functioning of the qualifying equipment. Id., see also Southwest Airlines, Inc. v. Bullock, 784 S.W.2d 563 (Tex.App.-Austin 1990, no writ). However, the Comptroller has also distinguished a “component part” from an “assemblage of multiple items.” See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,650 (2011). An integrated group of manufacturing and processing machines and ancillary equipment that together operate to produce a product are not for that reason a single item of manufacturing equipment. Id.

 

Per taxability guidance, “Peripheral supports that are component parts of qualifying manufacturing equipment (i.e., the legs of a machine) qualify for exemption. Supports or platforms that house or hold several pieces of equipment are not a part or component of any one piece of equipment (i.e., the framework of a plant) and do not qualify for exemption. Structural steel that becomes a component part of non-qualifying equipment does not qualify for exemption.” STAR Document No. 200002044L (February 11, 2000). Considering this guidance along with applicable statutes, Rules, and Comptroller jurisprudence, the Comptroller has held on multiple occasions that certain purchases asserted to be peripheral supports or a single item equipment eligible for the manufacturing exemption are not eligible because they are not component parts of a single item of equipment used in the manufacturing process. See, e.g. Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,284 (2011); 48,150 (2009); 40,495 (2002).

 

Claimants argue that because a solar farm without a tracking system to move the solar panels is not financially viable in Texas, the tracking system is necessary to manufacturing, rendering the COMPANY C purchases eligible for the manufacturing exemption. However, financial viability is not necessary to the manufacturing process and does not cause it. Because the COMPANY C system does not cause the conversion of sunlight into electricity, the system is not necessary or essential to the manufacturing, processing, or fabrication of electricity.

 

Furthermore, Claimants contend, “The entire solar array, not its individual components, is the ‘property’ that is exempt under section 151.318(a)(2), and this Court should apply the test to the whole array, not its subcomponents.” [ENDNOTE 5] In other words, Petitioner argues that the entire solar array is a single item of exempt manufacturing equipment. In the alternative, but related to this argument, Petitioner contends that the tracker system is eligible for the manufacturing exemption because it is an integral component of the larger solar array. However, Claimants’ positions are contrary to Comptroller jurisprudence establishing that an assemblage of multiple items does not render each item in the assemblage eligible for the manufacturing exemption. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,650 (2011). Hence, Claimants’ contentions that remaining contested refund amounts should be exempt as a component part or peripheral part of the solar panel that produces electricity or a single item of manufacturing equipment that includes the solar panel should be denied.

 

4. Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4)

 

The following items are exempted from the taxes imposed by this chapter if sold, leased, or rented to, or stored, used, or consumed by a manufacturer: actuators, steam production equipment and its fuel, in-process flow through tanks, cooling towers, generators, heat exchangers, transformers and the switches, breakers, capacitor banks, regulators, relays, reclosers, fuses, interruptors, reactors, arrestors, resistors, insulators, instrument transformers, and telemetry units that are related to the transformers, electronic control room equipment, computerized control units, pumps, compressors, and hydraulic units, that are used to power, supply, support, or control equipment that qualifies for exemption under Subdivision (2) or (5). Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4).

 

Though neither the statute nor the corresponding Comptroller Rule defines an actuator; however, the Comptroller has used the ordinary meaning of an actuator in Comptroller’s Decision, “An actuator is a mechanical device for moving or controlling something.” Comptroller’s Decision No. 45,162 (2007). To qualify for the claimed exemption, Claimant must first establish that the items are actuators or telemetry units as those terms are used in Section 151.318(a)(4), and second, that the items power, supply, support or control equipment that are exempted under Section 151.318(a)(2). Id.

 

Staff agreed to refund amounts under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4) based on an agreement that some items are actuators or telemetry units related to computerized control units that are used to power, supply, support, or control equipment that qualifies for exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2), namely solar panels. Agreed refund amounts are shown in Staff’s Exhibits 4 and 5 and include the Planetary Gear Brushless Motor/Planetary Gear Brushless Motor, Sensor-less, and telemetry units such as the SPC 150-A-WS/SPC 150-B-Safe Harbor, NCU2.0 OTS Enclosure.

 

Petitioner has not provided sufficient evidence to establish that any other requested purchases are eligible for a tax exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4) as is its burden.

 

5. Texas Tax Code § 151.318(b)(1)

 

The manufacturing exemption includes chemicals, catalysts, and other materials that are used during a manufacturing, processing, or fabrication operation to produce or induce a chemical or physical change, to remove impurities, or to make the product more marketable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(b)(1).

 

The plain language of the statute states that a catalyst must be used during manufacturing, processing, or fabrication to be eligible for the manufacturing exemption. Even though the COMPANY C system optimizes the opportunity for the solar panel to have maximum output, it is not involved in the conversion of sunlight to electricity, and the system is not eligible for the manufacturing exemption.

 

6. Tax Rate

 

A tax is imposed on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051(a). The sales tax rate is 6.25 percent of the sales price of the taxable item sold. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051(b). Local sales and use taxes are capped at a maximum rate of two percent. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.334(a)(27); (f).

 

Claimants contend they remitted more than 8.25% to their vendors for tax and are due a refund of the amounts in excess of 8.25%. To support their contention, Claimants provided purchase invoices showing tax charged at 6.25%. Claimants contends that the invoices represent an overcollection of tax; however, the invoices and record evidence do not establish why tax was paid or that tax in excess of 8.25% on a taxable sale was paid and remitted to the Comptroller. Record evidence also does not establish a specific amount of tax overpaid. Hence, Claimants have not met their evidentiary burden to show that they are due a refund for overpaid tax. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.325; see also 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26.

 

Because Claimants did not meet their evidentiary burden to establish by clear and convincing evidence that the contested items are eligible for the manufacturing exemption or by preponderance of the evidence that other refund amounts are due, the ALJ recommends that Claimants’ contentions be denied accept as agreed to by Staff.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	COMPANY A (Claimant 1) is a utility-scale solar power station in COUNTY, Texas, and COMPANY B (Claimant 2) is its corporate affiliate. Claimants operate a commercial solar farm. Modules and semiconductors in the solar panels absorb sunlight and convert sunlight into electricity.
	
	
	The solar panels are assembled into an array with a tracker system and other equipment. Claimants’ solar farm contains multiple arrays. Claimants purchase components of the tracker system from its vendor, COMPANY C. The array is custom designed and assembled on site.
	
	
	A tracker system is the grouping of components integrated into a solar array that allows the solar panels to move.
	
	
	The primary purpose of the movement is to maximize the exposure of the solar panels to sunlight, increasing the amount of sunlight converted into electricity by tracking the sun in the sky and adjusting the position accordingly.
	
	
	A secondary purpose is safety in that moving the solar panels enables protection during severe weather conditions, including hail, high winds, and flooding.
	
	
	COMPANY C uses latitude, longitude, and date to determine when the sun will rise and fall and what position would be optimal to increase irradiance.
	
	
	The commercial solar farm is comprised of an assembly of rows of arrays, including solar panels, that would move in unison. Each row has its own controller and receives information form a COMPANY C control unit.
	
	
	The position of the module in the solar panel is crucial to maximize output, or irradiance. A module pointing directly at the sun increases the footprint of irradiance.
	
	
	The goal of the COMPANY C system is to make more electricity and that without a tracker system, the commercial solar farm would not be financially viable and it would endure a higher cost to produce electricity on a per watt level.
	
	
	The COMPANY C system facilitates movement of the solar panels and is not needed for the solar panels to function.
	
	
	Mr. INDIVIDUAL C works as Claimant 2’s Director of Operations and Maintenance. Claimant 2 owns and operates several solar farms and Mr. INDIVIDUAL C manages third-party operators, on-site personnel, and work performed at the site. Mr. INDIVIDUAL C has worked in the solar farm industry for almost a decade and he has developed solar farms from the early stages through construction. He has owned and operated several of them. Mr. INDIVIDUAL C manages the COMPANY A project and checks production on it every morning.
	
	
	Professor INDIVIDUAL D is the CHAIR of the CHEMICAL ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT at UNIVERSITY. He is the Director of the ENERGY INSTITUTE.
	
	
	Claimants requested a refund of sales and use tax erroneously paid on purchases to vendors, namely COMPANY C, for the period of January 1, 2019, through October 31, 2021.
	
	
	Staff conducted a verification of Claimants’ refund claim. The auditor reviewed Claimants’ purchase invoices, general ledger accounts, COMPANY C’s sales and use tax returns filed with the Comptroller. The auditor also reached out to COMPANY C for information regarding its sales to Claimants.
	
	
	In February 2023, Staff denied the refund claims in full.
	
	
	Claimants timely filed a request for a hearing.
	
	
	Staff referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On October 22, 2024, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Keneshia Washington issued an Order Scheduling Hearing on the Merits, setting out the pre-hearing requirements.
	
	
	On October 30, 2024, Staff issued Claimants Notices of Hearing. The notices contained a statement of the date, time, and location of the hearing; the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	The hearing convened in person at the SOAH offices in Austin, Texas on April 29, 2025.
	
	
	The record closed on May 20, 2025, after Staff and Claimants filed written closing arguments, a refund schedule indicating agreed amounts and remaining amounts in contention for each hearing, and exhibits admitted to the record during the hearing via Texas E-File.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over these matters. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided a proper and timely notice of hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.105.
	
	
	If the Comptroller finds that an amount of tax, penalty, or interest has been unlawfully or erroneously collected, the Comptroller shall credit or refund the amount. Tex. Tax Code § 111.104(a).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing, a Claimant must produce contemporaneous records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a); see also Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041.
	
	
	A taxpayer has the burden to prove error in an assessment or refund denial by a preponderance of the evidence. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions by source records, and factual assertions in pleadings are not evidence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,587 (2015) and 112,607 (2019); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 115,999 (2020), citing to Baker v. Bullock, 529 S.W.2d 279 (Tex. Civ. App.— Austin 1975, writ ref’d n.r.e.); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 107,916 (2013).
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	When tax is imposed on tangible personal property the taxing entity’s prima facie burden of proof is easily met because, unless an exemption applies, all sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 116,506 (2020).
	
	
	A taxpayer has the burden to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the taxpayer or a transaction qualifies for an exemption. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c).
	
	
	Clear and convincing evidence demonstrates the thing to be proved is highly probable. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,773 (2011).
	
	
	Statutory exemptions are strictly construed and all doubts concerning exemption claims are resolved in favor of the taxing authority. See North Alamo Water Supply Corp. v. Willacy County Appraisal Dist., 804 S.W.2d 894, 899 (Tex. 1991); Sharp v. Tyler Pipe Indus. Inc., 919 S.W.2d 157, 161 (Tex. App.—Austin 1996, writ denied); Sharp v. Chevron Chem. Co., 924 S.W.2d 429, 432 (Tex. App.—Austin 1996, writ denied).
	
	
	The statute provides an exemption for tangible personal property directly used or consumed in or during the actual manufacturing, processing, or fabrication of tangible personal property for ultimate sale if the use or consumption of the property is necessary or essential to the manufacturing, processing, or fabrication operation and directly makes or causes a chemical or physical change to the product being manufactured, processed, or fabricated for ultimate sale. Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2)(A).
	
	
	Manufacturing includes each operation beginning with the first stage in the production of tangible personal property and ending with the completion of tangible personal property. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(9).
	
	
	Completion of production means the tangible personal property has all the physical properties, including packaging, if any, that it has when transferred by the manufacturer to another. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(9)(A).
	
	
	Processing and fabrication are two activities that are performed during manufacturing. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(9)(B).
	
	
	Processing is defined as “the physical application of the materials and labor necessary to modify or to change the characteristics of tangible personal property.” 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(10).
	
	
	Fabrication is defined as “to make, build, create, produce, or assemble components of tangible personal property, or to make tangible personal property work in a new or different manner.” 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.300(a)(5).
	
	
	Because the COMPANY C System does not cause the conversion of sunlight into electricity, the COMPANY C System is not eligible for the manufacturing exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(2)(A).
	
	
	The parties agree that the solar panel is a piece of manufacturing equipment used to convert sunlight into electricity. See State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document No. 201907005L (July 8, 2019) (explaining, “Solar modules/panels used to convert sunlight into electricity for ultimate sale qualify for exemption under Section 151.318(a)(2). They are single items of manufacturing equipment. Components of such modules/panels also qualify for exemption.”).
	
	
	The executive director or commission, as appropriate, shall after publication of the National Electrical Code by the National Fire Protection Association every three years, adopt the revised National Electrical Code as the electrical code for the state. Tex. Occupations Code § 1305.101(a)(2).
	
	
	No part of the Texas Tax Code requires the Comptroller to adopt the definition of a solar array published in the National Electrical Code.
	
	
	The determination of whether the contested purchases of the COMPANY C system constitute a component part of manufacturing equipment is a fact- driven question. See, e.g. Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 111,253 (2017), 105,258 (2011), 44,820 (2005), and 44,299 (2006).
	
	
	Although the statute does not contain the phrase “component part of the manufacturing equipment,” the agency construes Section 151.318(a)(2) as including the language. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,253 (2017); 44,820 (2005). In order for an item to qualify as a component part of manufacturing equipment, it must be reasonably essential to the functioning of the qualifying equipment. Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,253 (2017); 44,820 (2005), see also Southwest Airlines, Inc. v. Bullock, 784 S.W.2d 563 (Tex.App.-Austin 1990, no writ).
	
	
	The Comptroller has also distinguished a “component part” from an “assemblage of multiple items.” See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,650 (2011).
	
	
	An integrated group of manufacturing and processing machines and ancillary equipment that together operate to produce a product are not for that reason a single item of manufacturing equipment. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,650 (2011).
	
	
	Peripheral supports that are component parts of qualifying manufacturing equipment (i.e., the legs of a machine) qualify for exemption. Supports or platforms that house or hold several pieces of equipment are not a part or component of any one piece of equipment (i.e., the framework of a plant) and do not qualify for exemption. Structural steel that becomes a component part of non-qualifying equipment does not qualify for exemption. STAR Document No. 200002044L (February 11, 2000).
	
	
	The Comptroller has held on multiple occasions that certain purchases asserted to be peripheral supports or a single item equipment eligible for the manufacturing exemption are not eligible because they are not component parts of a single item of equipment used in the manufacturing process. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,284 (2011); 48,150 (2009); 40,495 (2002).
	
	
	Because the COMPANY C system does not cause the conversion of sunlight into electricity, the system is not necessary or essential to the manufacturing, processing, or fabrication of electricity.
	
	
	Claimants’ contentions that remaining contested refund amounts should be exempt as a component part or peripheral part of the solar panel that produces electricity or a single item of manufacturing equipment that includes the solar panel should be denied.
	
	
	The following items are exempted from the taxes imposed by this chapter if sold, leased, or rented to, or stored, used, or consumed by a manufacturer: actuators, steam production equipment and its fuel, in-process flow through tanks, cooling towers, generators, heat exchangers, transformers and the switches, breakers, capacitor banks, regulators, relays, reclosers, fuses, interruptors, reactors, arrestors, resistors, insulators, instrument transformers, and telemetry units that are related to the transformers, electronic control room equipment, computerized control units, pumps, compressors, and hydraulic units, that are used to power, supply, support, or control equipment that qualifies for exemption under Subdivision (2) or (5). Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4).
	
	
	Though neither the statute nor the corresponding Comptroller Rule defines an actuator; however, the Comptroller has used the ordinary meaning of an actuator in Comptroller’s Decision, “An actuator is a mechanical device for moving or controlling something.” Comptroller’s Decision No. 45,162 (2007).
	
	
	To qualify for the claimed exemption, Claimant must first establish that the items are actuators or telemetry units as those terms are used in Section 151.318(a)(4), and second, that the items power, supply, support or control equipment that are exempted under Section 151.318(a)(2). Comptroller’s Decision No. 45,162 (2007).
	
	
	Petitioner has not provided sufficient evidence to establish that any other requested purchases are eligible for a tax exemption under Texas Tax Code § 151.318(a)(4) as is its burden.
	
	
	The manufacturing exemption includes chemicals, catalysts, and other materials that are used during a manufacturing, processing, or fabrication operation to produce or induce a chemical or physical change, to remove impurities, or to make the product more marketable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.318(b)(1).
	
	
	Because the COMPANY C system is not involved in the conversion of sunlight to electricity, the system is not eligible for the manufacturing exemption.
	
	
	A tax is imposed on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051(a).
	
	
	The sales tax rate is 6.25 percent of the sales price of the taxable item sold. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051(b).
	
	
	Local sales and use taxes are capped at a maximum rate of two percent. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.334(a)(27); (f).
	
	
	The invoices and record evidence do not establish why tax was paid or that tax in excess of 8.25% on a taxable sale was paid and remitted to the Comptroller.
	
	
	Record evidence does not establish a specific amount of tax overpaid.
	
	
	Claimants have not met their evidentiary burden to show that they are due a refund for overpaid tax. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.325; see also 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26.
	
	
	Because Claimants did not meet their evidentiary burden to establish by clear and convincing evidence that the contested items are eligible for the manufacturing exemption or by preponderance of the evidence that other refund amounts are due, the ALJ recommends that Claimants’ contentions be denied except as agreed to by Staff.
	


 

Signed July 18, 2025.

 

Keneshia Washington

Presiding Administrative Law Judge
